**PRE-MEETING BRIEFING — STUDENT 344199**
*Professional Advisor Use Only | Confidential*

---

Student 344199 (Male, 35–55, Module DDD) has been flagged following a significant and rapid drop in engagement: despite being active across 77 days of his programme, his participation has sharply declined in recent weeks, he has withdrawn from one module, and his active engagement has noticeably tapered — a pattern that often signals something meaningful has shifted in a student's life or motivation. His academic performance is solid at around 71%, which means this is less likely to be about academic capability and more likely to reflect something happening outside of — or alongside — his studies.

In the meeting, listen carefully for **competing life pressures** such as work demands, caring responsibilities, or financial strain (common for mature students in this age band), and also **signs of disengagement from a sense of purpose or belonging** — for instance, whether he still feels the qualification is achievable or relevant to his goals.

A trust-building opening question to consider: *"It's great to connect with you — how are you finding things at the moment, both on the programme and more generally?"*

If the conversation surfaces anything beyond academic or pastoral support — such as indicators of significant personal distress, mental health concerns, or crisis — please follow your institution's referral pathway to student wellbeing or counselling services, and document accordingly; your role here is to listen, support, and connect, not to assess or advise beyond your remit.

**PRE-MEETING BRIEFING — STUDENT 650414**
*Confidential | For Therapist Use Only*

---

Student 650414 (male, under 35, CCC module) has been flagged following a significant and rapid withdrawal from academic engagement — he was active across 38 days but his participation has declined sharply, he has dropped two modules, and his assessment performance has fallen to effectively zero, suggesting something meaningful shifted for him during this period rather than a gradual drift. In your session, it will be especially important to listen for signs of sudden life disruption — such as a personal loss, relationship breakdown, financial stress, or a health concern — that may have triggered this withdrawal, as well as any language that hints at hopelessness, feeling overwhelmed, or a sense that continuing simply doesn't feel possible or worthwhile. A warm opening question to build trust might be: *"Before we talk about anything academic, I just want to check in — how have you actually been doing lately, as a person?"* Given the depth and speed of the disengagement pattern, if the conversation surfaces themes of self-harm, suicidal ideation, or acute crisis, please follow your institution's safeguarding escalation pathway and ensure a same-day handoff to emergency mental health support rather than scheduling a follow-up.

**PRE-MEETING BRIEFING — CONFIDENTIAL**
**Student ID:** 529525 | **Module:** DDD | **Urgency:** Immediate
**Prepared for:** Class Advisor | **For Advisor Use Only**

---

**Why This Student Was Flagged**
This student has shown a significant and consistent withdrawal from her studies over a 42-day period — she has been active on only 11 of those days, has dropped one module, and her assessment performance is averaging 27/100, suggesting that disengagement and academic difficulty have been building together for some time rather than emerging suddenly.

**What to Listen For**
Listen carefully for any signs that external pressures — such as personal circumstances, financial stress, health concerns, or caring responsibilities — may be driving her withdrawal, as these would require a coordinated support response beyond academic advising; also listen for whether she feels she belongs on the course or has quietly begun to disengage emotionally, as this will shape how you frame any next steps with her.

**Suggested Opening Question**
A good way to open warmly and without pressure might be: *"Thanks so much for coming in — I just wanted to check in and hear how things have been going for you lately, in your own words."*
